data.title: Humana: Implied Volatility Creating A Viable Investment Case
data.description:
In our previous coverage of Humana Inc. (NYSE:HUM), we urged investors to be cautious as the earnings estimates were far from reality.

So what does this mean? It means that those two numbers down there are likely to be completely wrong.

We are going out on a limb here and saying even 2025 will be completely far away from estimates as HUM works hard to get the star ratings up. We will have to wait to do more modeling on this after the full impact is clarified (likely during the Q3-2024 conference call), but we expect 2025 earnings to be flat ($14.50-$17 per share) relative to 2024. We would expect a big drop in 2026 and this is what the recent selloff is about. Our guesstimate is that HUM will struggle to clear $12.00 of earnings per share.

Source: 2026 Earnings Likely To Be Crushed

That was the thesis and this has been the result.

Let's look at how the numbers are shaping and see if we can find a way to play this.

2025 Estimates

Analysts have finally gotten onboard with the headwinds and have downgraded the estimates at a rapid clip since our last work. At $16.35, the EPS estimates are actually now within the band we suggested they would fall within.

Estimates are beginning to stabilize, and we can see there were actually a few revisions higher as well. The stock has digested most of that bad news and the bears have not made much progress despite that.

2026 Estimates

This was where we expected the most meat on the bone for the bears. The EPS estimates were at $25.53 at the time. $14.50 seems far more reasonable though we see more downside risk here all the way to $12.00 per share.

With both numbers though closer to their potential troughs than they were 8 months back, can we make the bull case?

Outlook

Health insurers have always been a very low-margin business. If you compare what they make to pharmaceuticals and biotechnology companies, you will understand what we are talking about. You can see below where Pfizer Inc. (PFE), Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) and Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD) stand relative to UnitedHealth Group Incorporated (UNH) and Centene Corporation (CNC).

Our protagonist, Humana, is missing here because, for some reason, YCharts was not ready to pull up this data. However, we can verify that the company averages gross margins generally close to 15%. While those are smaller numbers than most healthcare companies, it still results in a lot of government money, especially when you have such a huge Medicaid concentration.

The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services made some major announcements that were aimed at reducing potential overpayments to private insurers and even claw back some amounts made in previous years.

The Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services has introduced sweeping reforms aimed at curbing anti-competitive practices, enhancing transparency, and promoting equity. Key changes impacting Humana include:

  1. Star Ratings Controversy: CMS revised its methodology for calculating MA Star Ratings in 2025, drastically lowering Humana's scores. For instance, its flagship H5216 plan plummeted from 4.5 to 3.5 stars, leaving only 25% of its membership in 4-star or higher plans—a stark contrast to 94% in 2024. This downgrade has severe financial consequences, as quality bonus payments now evaporate.

  2. Agent Compensation Overhaul: CMS capped agent commissions at $411 per enrollment to prevent steering toward high-commission plans. While this limits unfair incentives, it may reduce sales force motivation, exacerbating Humana's membership decline.

  3. Data Privacy and Marketing Restrictions: Prohibitions on sharing beneficiary data without explicit consent and stricter oversight of TPMO contracts aim to curb aggressive marketing.

Source: AInvest

The new measures aim to claw back about $9 billion in estimated Medicaid payments (this amount applies to Humana alone) made between 2018 and 2024. This number was based on an extrapolation of the 2023 numbers, though we doubt anyone has any certainty as to their validity. The hits don't stop for the company just there. The current bill in the Senate designed to cut taxes for the upper brackets also comes with spending cuts for Medicaid. While the timelines are uncertain and the bill still faces a ton of opposition, even from Republicans, this creates more trauma to the growth story. The growth story we are referring to is the fairy tale that Humana's investors will live happily ever after as the earnings go vertical in the back half of this decade.

Based on where things stand, we see three reasons why that will just not happen. The first being that the lower the actual earnings at the front end (2026-2027) the lower the earnings at the back end. This is simply from a buyback perspective as the less shares Humana retires, the less the EPS will rise during 2028-2029, all other things being equal. The second reason is that there will be at least some clawback and that will require Humana to go into balance sheet repair mode. The rating agencies are watching carefully and there are good reasons why Fitch has their outlook of "Negative".

The Rating Outlook on HUM's 'BBB+' Long-Term Issuer Default Rating was revised to Negative from Stable on May 3, 2024. HUM maintains adequate capitalization and leverage, which is a moderately weighted factor in its ratings. While HUM's financial leverage ratio was generally within Fitch's expectations of 40% or below at YE 2024, margin pressure due to elevated utilization in the senior population in 2024 resulted in a debt/EBITDA ratio near 4.6x for the year, which is significantly above Fitch's expectations for the company's current ratings.

Fitch will continue to monitor HUM's operating performance as 2025 unfolds to determine whether initiatives implemented by management, including exits from Medicare Advantage plans with high benefit ratios and benefit adjustments to remaining plans, will be sufficient to materially improve earnings-based financial leverage measures within a timeframe consistent with Fitch's typical rating horizon. Fitch will also continue to monitor developments around litigation filed by HUM in October 2024 against certain government agencies related to the company's 2025 Star Ratings.

Source: Fitch.

So no buybacks and all those back-end estimates continue to incorporate a lot of that.

Finally, we see Humana's debt rollover posing a material threat with higher rates. The combination are more bloated balance sheet with potentially lower credit ratings means that you could be looking at sub $20 per share earnings as far as the eye can see.

Verdict

When we first wrote about Humana in 2024, we suggested investors holster their bullish views as Humana was not as cheap as it looked.

The rationale then was that while it looked "cheap" from a P/E standpoint, price-to-sales multiples told a different story. Margin reversion would be nasty for Humana's model and that has been the story for the last 18 months. The good part is that we are approaching the troughs of 2008 and 2000 on this multiple.

The bad part is that an exact match would create another 50% downside. So the risk remains incredibly high that we could see mediocre returns from here in the very best scenarios. The good part here for investors is that options are actually offering a good risk-reward here, and you just have to get the thesis half-right to make money. Here we a referring to longer-dated options and not the monthly ones that buy-write funds sell blindly. The $230 calls offer over 16% annualized returns and all that needs to happen is that Humana needs to do modestly well and stabilize its operations.

On the downside, your breakeven is $179.00, which, we think, is a good price to actually get long Humana. More aggressive investors could consider selling 2 calls at this strike for every 100 shares. That would mean that would actually go short 100 shares at a net price of $323 if Humana holds over $230 by January 2027. But this strategy would also reduce their cost basis down to $131 if Humana holds under $230 18 months from now.

Please note that this is not financial advice. It may seem like it, sound like it, but surprisingly, it is not. Investors are expected to do their own due diligence and consult a professional who knows their objectives and constraints.

data.title: Silver Is Breaking Out - Here's What You Need To Know (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

Silver futures have broken out, delivering a strong bullish signal—but I’m still looking for additional confirmation to strengthen the case.

I’ve been consistently bullish on silver and have been pointing out the key signals to watch for a breakout from its prolonged consolidation of the past year. I’ve repeatedly said the main trigger would be a decisive, high-volume move above two critical resistance zones: $32 to $33 and then $34 to $35.

While many grew weary of waiting and doubted it would happen, it finally did yesterday—which is incredibly exciting. That’s why I’m publishing this quick technical update to show you where silver stands now and what I’m looking for next to confirm that the bull market is fully underway.

The primary form of silver I monitor is COMEX silver futures, as they tend to respect $1 increments as key support and resistance levels and, unlike the spot price, also display trading volume—an essential indicator of conviction and institutional money flows.

Remarkably, silver finally broke through the critical $35 threshold yesterday on strong volume, delivering a clear and decisive bullish signal. This signal remains valid as long as silver holds above that level. I believe this breakout sets the stage for a rapid move to $40, $50, $60, and beyond, as outlined in my detailed report linked in the first paragraph of this update.

One of the immediate catalysts behind yesterday's breakout is China’s rare earth metals export curbs. However, the reality is that this breakout was inevitable—and it is primarily technical in nature. Silver has been like a coiled spring, simply waiting for a spark to unleash its momentum, and that’s exactly what we’re seeing now.

In addition, precious metals have been gaining support from a combination of recent developments: Moody’s downgrade of U.S. debt, continued weakness in the U.S. dollar, escalating tariff tensions between the U.S. and China, the Trump administration’s controversial new budget, and rising political dysfunction—fueled further by Elon Musk’s public falling-out with President Trump.

As I’ve noted before, silver’s frustrating consolidation over the past year has reminded me of gold’s prolonged range from 2020 to 2024—a period I said would eventually give way to a major bull market once gold broke above its $2,000 to $2,100 resistance zone, which it ultimately did.

I believe silver is now following a similar path, and yesterday's breakout has a very strong chance of marking the beginning of a powerful new bull market.

I’ve also developed a proprietary tool called the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI), designed to help validate silver’s price action and filter out potential false breakouts.

The SSPI is calculated as the average of gold and copper prices, with copper scaled by a factor of 540 to prevent gold from dominating the index. Interestingly, even though silver isn’t part of the calculation, the SSPI closely tracks its movements.

I’ve been highlighting how the SSPI has been trading within a consolidation range between 2,800 and 3,000. A breakout above the 3,000 level would serve as a strong bullish confirmation for silver.

Yesterday, the SSPI initially rallied—driven by a sharp upward move in copper—but as copper gave back much of its gains, the SSPI failed to close above the key 3,000 level. Despite that, momentum remains strong, and I believe there’s a high probability the SSPI will make another breakout attempt soon. This, along with a decisive breakout in euro-priced silver, is one of the final confirmations I’m watching for.

The copper futures chart below shows how prices tested—but failed to break above—the key $5.00 to $5.20 resistance zone, which held back both the SSPI and silver from achieving even further gains yesterday. For more on my outlook for copper and why I’m bullish on it alongside silver, read my recent report.

In addition to silver itself, I’m also highly bullish on silver mining stocks and ETFs, which I expect to deliver even larger gains in the coming bull market due to their leverage to the price of silver. Large silver miners, as tracked by the SIL ETF, have been performing strongly after breaking out of a long-term triangle pattern that dates all the way back to 2011.

SIL is now approaching a critical resistance zone between $48 and $52—a level that has repeatedly capped rallies since 2016. I believe a decisive breakout above this zone, especially in tandem with a confirmed silver bull market, will trigger an explosive move higher in silver mining stocks. I’m watching closely and very excited about the potential upside.

Silver junior mining stocks, as tracked by the SILJ ETF, are even more volatile than their larger-cap counterparts—and offer even greater profit potential during bull markets.

Currently, SILJ remains within a long-term triangle pattern that dates back to 2013. I believe that once it breaks out of this formation, both SILJ and the broader junior silver mining space are poised to surge dramatically—offering significant upside for those positioned ahead of the move.

While not an absolute necessity, one final confirmation of the silver bull market would be a decisive breakdown in the U.S. dollar, which historically trades inversely to commodities—including precious metals.

As I’ve explained recently, the key level to watch on the U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) is 100. The index is now trading below that threshold, giving it a clear bearish bias. The next critical level is 98; if that support breaks, I believe the index will quickly move toward the next major support at 90. Such a move would act as rocket fuel for the bull market in gold, silver, and mining stocks.

To summarize, yesterday's nascent breakout in silver is both encouraging and exciting. However, I’m looking for additional confirmation before calling it a full-scale breakout. Specifically, I want to see silver break out in euros, the Synthetic Silver Price Index (SSPI) move decisively higher, and the U.S. Dollar Index break down further.

Once those pieces fall into place, I truly believe the major silver bull market I’ve been calling for—and that many of us have been waiting on—will finally be underway. As always, I’ll continue to keep you updated.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: May Jobs Report Surprises, Despite Mixed JOLTS And ADP
data.description:
Introduction

The May Jobs Report released by the US BLS on June 6th was reported as "a healthy gain," with the report showing an add of 139K jobs, higher than the 126K consensus estimate.

The overall picture is still looking relatively good from this perspective, especially with the unemployment rate (both U-3 and U-6) staying at the same level month-to-month. The current level of unemployment is considered "natural," or the level at which we should expect in a functioning economy, as it is normal to have some level of churn in labor markets.

Area most improved in the report:

  • Healthcare +62,000 jobs
  • Leisure & Hospitality +48,000 jobs

Area hit the worst in the report:

  • Manufacturing -8,000 jobs
  • Federal employment -22,000 jobs

Although, it should be of note that the loss in federal jobs was offset by state and government jobs, which added 21,000. This leads to the total "government" category to a loss of 1,000 jobs, not necessarily the headline "22,000" that I saw reported alongside the release of this data.

Just like last time, the BLS did disappoint with heavy revisions to past jobs numbers:

The change in total nonfarm payroll employment for March was revised down by 65,000, from +185,000 to +120,000, and the change for April was revised down by 30,000, from +177,000 to +147,000. With these revisions, employment in March and April combined is 95,000 lower than previously reported.

A largely negative highlight on the report: temporary agency positions fell by 20,000. This is a particularly negative sign because firms are usually less likely to cut temporary hiring over permanent hiring. This could be a sign of early softening, but it's still too soon to tell. It could also be noise and the trend could correct in the coming months.

Impact of the May Jobs Report

The job’s report was ultimately good, but only in its own context, as rate changes from the Fed are likely to be decided on by inflation levels, with the labor market acting as a canary.

I see this report as dispelling some discord brought on by other jobs data this week, that has caused markets to send mixed signals. Just this past week, there were two other jobs reports that are worth taking a look at, to see how they diverge from the BLS figures that are normally the largest headline, and move markets the most.

Following the BLS report on June 6th, the US major indices rallied nearly 1% across the board:

It's important for us to look at that other data and take a look at the reason there was a lower expectation for this BLS report than what we got. The rest of the data this past week wasn't as pretty.

Other Jobs Data Has Been Divergent

There's been a notable divergence in the jobs reports from this past week, looking at both the JOLTS and ADP reports, released within days of this latest jobs report from the BLS. The data tells us different parts of the overall story of the economy, and highlight the differences in data collection and survey participation between sectors in the labor market. It's important to get all sides of a story when it comes to the economy, as we can often miss the truth by focusing in on one data point too deeply.

JOLTS Data

Here is the JOLTS data from this week, where its April hires figures blew away the rest of the year for hires, indicating a strong demand for labor, and the strongest month YTD.

Figures are measured in thousands.

That dataset also showed us that layoffs were on the rise as well, and has risen again to similar levels to back in February, despite a dip in the March figures released last month.

Openings are down to the third-lowest point in a while, indicating how tight the market is getting as layoffs and discharges rise.

We can also see this "tightness" in the quits figure, as fewer and fewer people are quitting their jobs from YTD figures, more in line with the end of last year, and lower than the 2024 average.

When we combine all the separations into one category, we get a figure that is nearly in line with the hiring figure, showing us that "churn" that we see in the BLS report as well, but a "churn" that is fairly stagnant.

ADP Employment Data

The worst report of the week was by far the ADP survey, where private payrolls sat at a 2-year low, down to 37K.

Just like in the BLS report (corroboration is a good thing between these reports), leisure and hospitality were one of the strongest sectors.

Information jobs, largely tech roles, have been on the rise after a sharp downturn in April, and remain mixed overall.

However, and this was particularly negative on the ADP report, we saw a very low figure for construction in May; not as low as March, but this makes March less of an outlier and more of an indicator for the new trend.

Manufacturing also disappointed on the report, with a loss of 3k jobs. This does come after a large correction and massive swings in job counts in the sector this year.

Conclusion

The May Jobs Report from the BLS confirms and denies some suppositions the market has been making about the labor market, given the mixed data we saw in the JOLTS and ADP reports this week. Ultimately, the BLS report casts a brighter light on the economy than the other reports. We have seen a few patterns emerge that I will be following:

  • Services are outperforming goods, e.g., leisure over manufacturing.
  • Layoffs increasing across the board, showing potential weakness.
  • Data divergence between job postings and hiring is telling of business sentiment.
    • They seem more eager to post jobs than to fill them, showing weakness.
  • State governments are absorbing many federal job losses, meaning the headline federal job losses are potentially not true government losses on the whole, just a movement from federal to state level positions.

I will be looking forward to the next Fed meeting on June 18th to see what comments the FOMC makes about the labor market. I expect them to be hawkish at this next meeting, and hold rates steady. This report won't be enough for them to cut, as the mixed signals don't show enough of a downtrend for them to worry just yet.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: Autolus: A Buy Before Key Data Readout, Potential Expansion Into Autoimmune Market
data.description:
Investment Overview

When I last covered Autolus Therapeutics plc (NASDAQ:AUTL) for Seeking Alpha back in September last year, it was to make a Bull case for its shares, based on the UK-based biotech's upcoming PDUFA date for its autologous CAR-T cell therapy, obecabtagene autoleucel ("obe-cel"), in patients with relapsed/refractory (r/r) adult B-cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia (B-ALL).

Unfortunately, in 2024 we witnessed the emergence of a worrying new trend of drug approvals creating more negative market sentiment than positive, and although Autolus reported on November 8th, 2024, that obe-cel had secured approval, the brand name AUCATZYL, after the briefest of spikes from ~$3.3 per share, to $4, shares began to lose value, sinking as low as $1.15 per share in April this year.

Quoting from Autolus' press release, here is how the company summarizes the data, from its pivotal FELIX study, that led to U.S. approval:

In the efficacy evaluable patients (n=65), 63% achieved overall complete remission (OCR1) which includes 51% of patients with CR at any time and 12% of patients with CRi at any time. The major efficacy outcome was complete remission within 3 months, which was achieved in 42% of patients, and the median duration of remission (DOR) was 14.1 months.

AUCATZYL showed low levels of Cytokine Release Syndrome (CRS), with 3% Grade 3 events, and no Grade 4 or 5 events. Grade ≥ 3 Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome (ICANS) was reported in 7% of patients. No REMS was required by the FDA for AUCATZYL.

At the end of last month, Autolus announced that AUCATZYL has been recommended for EU approval by the Committee for Medicinal Products for Human Use ("CHMP"), based on data from Felix discussed below:

In the pivotal cohort of patients, (cohort IIA (n=94)), the Complete Response/Complete Response with Incomplete Haematological Recovery (CR/CRi) for patients who received at least one infusion of obecabtagene autoleucel was 76.6%. The median response duration for all infused patients was 21.2 months. Median event-free survival (EFS) was 11.9 months and the estimated 6- and 12-month event-free survival rates were 65.4% and 49.5%, respectively.

The most common non-laboratory Grade 3 or higher adverse reactions were infections-pathogen unspecified (32%), febrile neutropenia (24%), and bacterial infectious disorders (11%). Cytokine release syndrome developed in 87 of the 127 patients (68.5%), with events of grade 3 or higher in three patients (2.4%). Immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome developed in 29 of the 127 patients (22.8%), with grade 3 or higher occurring in nine patients (7%).

The reason I am sharing summaries of both datasets is that they seemingly establish AUCATZYL as the best-in-class treatment in its field. Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD) Tecartus was approved to treat B-ALL in 2021, earning revenues of >$400m last year, based on a 65% complete remission and 13.6 months DoR. AUCATZYL's two data sets average out at a slightly superior CR rate and superior median DoR.

In terms of safety, Tecartus's study resulted in a 26% and 35% percentage of patients experiencing Grade 3 or higher adverse events related to cytokine release syndrome ("CRS") and neurologic events respectively. Seemingly, AUCATZYL's study has a much lower rate of Grade 3+ CRS at ~3%. Kymriah, also marketed and sold by Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD) is also approved for ALL, but only "for certain pediatric and young adult patients."

Autolus has apparently priced AUCATZYL higher than tecartus, at $525k, and analysts have speculated it could earn peak revenues of ~$300m per annum.

Autolus has invested heavily in developing AUCATZYL, and in a 70,000-square foot facility called the Nucleus, in Stevenage, United Kingdom, which the company says (in its 2024 annual report/10-K submission):

has a global reach, and can meet our near and mid-term clinical and commercial needs allowing ample time for expanding our manufacturing footprint...at full capacity, we expect the Nucleus facility to provide manufacturing capacity for approximately 2,000 batches annually.

Autolus has made losses of $(221m), $(208m), $(149m), and $(142m) across the past four years, working backward, hence, given the peak sales potential of its only commercial asset, perhaps its current share price of $2.3, and market cap valuation of $560m is warranted.

Nevertheless, in this note I plan to look a little deeper into the strengths, weaknesses, opportunities, and threats associated with this company, to see if buying stock today or continuing to hold stock if you already own it, is the right strategy.

Autolus - Q1 2025 In Review AUCATZYL's Strong Launch

For Q1 2025, Autolus reported AUCATZYL revenues of $9m, which was ahead of analysts' expectations, although the net loss of $(70.2m), or $(0.26) per share, was wider than expected. Autolus reported a cash position of $96m, with $421m of near-term cash, so even at the current burn rate, it would not be exhausted until 2027, we can speculate.

Autolus says it has reimbursement in place from commercial and government patients, and 39 treatment centers authorized to deliver AUCATZYL. The company's ambition is to have 60 centers online, which it says, "will give us approximately 90% access to patients across the U.S."

Nevertheless, it's clear that in order to succeed and grow as a business, Autolus needs to turn losses into profits, and one obvious way to do that is to earn more revenues.

Autolus has not provided any revenue - or expenses - guidance for 2025, so far as I am aware. Interestingly, analysts on the Q1 earnings call noted that Tecartus revenues in Q1 were ~$78m, down from $100m in the prior year, and asked if this impact could have been related to the launch of AUCATZYL?

Autolus' CEO Christian Itin speculated it would more likely be related to the mantle cell lymphoma market which tecartus is also approved in, where it faces increasing competition from Bristol-Myers Squibb Company's (BMY) Breyanzi. When asked what percentage of Tecartus' Q1 earnings would have come from which indication, Itin suggested it may have been 20-40% of ALL patients two years ago, but it was hard to know given Gilead does not share that data.

This is a long-winded way of suggesting that in its current market, AUCATZYL may do well to earn much more than ~$50m per quarter at its peak, even if it successfully takes market share away from Tecartus, but fortunately, Autolus has advanced plans to expand the number of indications the drug is approved for.

Autolus - Development Plans For AUCATZYL

The most obvious next target for Autolus and AUCATZYL is first-line ALL. As per CEO ITIN, again speaking on the Q1 earnings call.

What we do know from obe-cel and AUCATZYL is that the product when used in patients that are in earlier – in the earlier stages of relapsed or also patients that have lower tumor burden at the time of therapy, the patients do better with those profiles. We believe one of the key areas that I think is important to explore is the utility of the product ultimately in a frontline consolidation setting. We will start to explore – see the exploration of frontline settings through investigator-sponsored trials, and we’ll certainly consider our steps in addition to those.

In fact, Autolus may be able to target other B-cell malignancies - provided its strong efficacy and safety profile holds up in indications such as non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma ("NHL"), or diffuse large B-cell lymphoma ("DLBCL"), AUCATZYL may find itself able to challenge e.g., Gilead's Yescarta, another cell therapy which earned nearly $1.5bn in revenues in 2024.

Intriguingly, Autolus is touting a new long-term data readout from the FELIX study which will presented "mid-year" the company says, and which could help trigger a push into new liquid cancer indications. Pediatric ALL is another indication in which Autolus may have collected enough data to suggest an eventual approval is more likely than not.

As we can see above, besides oncology, Autolus believes AUCATZYL may have a shot at treating autoimmune conditions as well. Phase 1 data from a study in systemic lupus erythematosus ("SLE"), is expected in Q4, while three new Phase 1 studies are expected to initiate, in lupus nephritis, multiple sclerosis ("MS"), and AL amyloidosis.

This isn't unusual for a cell therapy company. Cabaletta Bio, Inc. (CABA), for example, pivoted into autoimmune, citing data from landmark studies conducted by Dr Georg Schett - who was also cited by Autolus' CEO on the Q1 earnings call - in which 8 out of 8 patients with SLE achieved Definition of Remission in SLE (DORIS) remission after a median follow-up of 15 months, after being treated with a cell therapy.

Cabaletta's progress has been slow, and its share price has suffered, so we may conclude that Autolus faces a real challenge in achieving success in this market, although it has shared data from its Phase 1 study in SLE, shown below:

CEO Itin stressed on the earnings call that patients in this study had more advanced disease than those treated in Dr Schett's study, but noted that, after only three months "very quick improvement" in inflammatory processes was observed, and that "three of the six patients did actually achieve renal complete remissions."

The CEO outlined plans for lupus and MS, noting that "obe-cel actually works very well across the blood-brain barrier," which is important in the treatment of MS. Autoimmune markets are vast, running into tens of billions of dollars, and therefore, if Autolus is successful with these studies, it would be reasonable to expect its valuation and share price to climb.

Concluding Thoughts - Autolus Retains My Faith Thanks To Strength Of Key Product

AUCATZYL is an ex-vivo cell therapy, and all such therapies take their toll on patients, who must undergo a form of chemotherapy in order for their cells to be extracted, and reinfused, which requires hospitalization.

On the plus side, AUCATZYL has demonstrated its ability to achieve durable complete remissions in patients and its treatment regime may be the most palatable among currently approved cell therapies. Autolus points out that AUCATZYL is the first CAR-T therapy to have been approved by the FDA with no Risk Evaluation and Mitigation Strategy ("REMS") program.

In order to be successful, I believe Autolus will need to secure further approvals for AUCATZYL, and we should note that, if obe-cel struggles to make the grade in other indications, there are no other drug candidates to fall back on, so there is a high level of "single asset" risk here.

Nevertheless, it seems to me that Autolus has brought a best-in-class product to market and that it has the funds both to build on an initially successful drug launch and fund studies in new indications.

Considering all of the above, I am awarding Autolus stock a 'Buy" rating before what could be a data readout from the FELIX study of real significance, paving the way for new approval shots in oncology markets, and more autoimmune data.

If Autolus can open up autoimmune markets, I can see its market cap valuation climbing >$1bn, but funding will be required, so I'd also expect Autolus to complete a fundraising if its share price does spike, which will be dilutive to investors, but shouldn't stop them securing a good return on investment.

data.title: Allspring Disciplined U.S. Core Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
FUND STRATEGY

  • Emphasizes undervalued companies that are likely to exceed earnings expectations.
  • Implements a disciplined quantitatively based strategy grounded in fundamental research; companies are evaluated based on factors related to valuation, quality, and momentum.
  • Consistently seeks to add value to the benchmark while actively managing portfolio risk using risk modeling and optimization tools.

Quarterly Review

  • The Allspring Disciplined U.S. Core Equity Fund (MUTF:EVSAX) returned -5.07% for the quarter. The benchmark S&P 500 Index (SP500, SPX) returned -4.27%.
  • For the three-month period, industrials, information technology (IT), and energy detracted from relative performance.
  • Utilities, communication services, and health care added to relative performance.

Market Review

After reaching a record high in February, the S&P 500 Index concluded a volatile first quarter of 2025 down 4.27%, snapping a streak of five consecutive quarters of positive returns for the index. During January, U.S. equities rallied on healthy corporate earnings and optimism over the pro-growth and deregulation initiatives of the Trump administration. However, by March, the S&P 500 Index fell into correction territory, as the momentum of U.S. equities quickly succumbed to volatile tariff policy headlines, persistent inflation, faltering tech leadership, softer corporate guidance, and recession fears.

Despite impending tariff concerns, U.S. inflation rates receded slightly in February. Price pressures eased modestly for energy, groceries, and housing, with annualized rates of U.S. headline and core Consumer Price Index inflation falling to 2.8% and 3.1%, respectively. The U.S. housing market remained stagnant due to limited affordability and inventory, with prospective buyers struggling to adjust to the “new normal” of mortgage rates between 6% and 7%.

In March, the Federal Open Market Committee maintained the federal funds rate at a target range of 4.25 to 4.50%. The Federal Reserve (Fed) paused for a second straight meeting following three consecutive rate cuts that began in September 2024. The Core Consumer Price Index (CPI) is a measure that examines the weighted average of prices of a basket of consumer goods and services, but excludes food and energy. It is calculated by taking price changes for each item in the predetermined basket of goods and averaging them. Changes in the Core CPI are used to assess price changes associated with the cost of living. The CPI is one of the most frequently used indicators for identifying periods of inflation or deflation. ATTRIBUTION ANALYSIS:

Performance attribution and sector returns are calculated using the Brinson-Fachler attribution model. As such, performance attribution calculations may differ from the fund's actual investment results. Common sources of attribution dispersion from official returns include: the use of end of day holdings and prices, the exclusion of fees, and the use of myriad pricing sources to accommodate different benchmark providers.

DEFINITION OF TERMS:

Alpha measures the difference between a fund's actual returns and its expected performance given its level of risk (as measured by beta).

Beta measures fund volatility relative to general market movements. It is a standardized measure of systematic risk in comparison with a specified index. The benchmark beta is 1.00 by definition.

Downside capture ratio measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of negative returns. During periods of negative benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

Information ratio measures the consistency of excess return (return in excess of a benchmark). This value is determined by taking the annualized excess return over a benchmark (style benchmark by default) and dividing it by the standard deviation of excess return.

Price/Earnings (P/E) is the price of a share of a stock divided by earnings per share, usually calculated using the last year’s earnings.

R-squared is a measure of how similar a fund’s historical performance has been to that of the benchmark. The measure ranges from 0.0, which means that the fund’s performance bears no relationship to the performance of the index, to 1.0, which means that the fund’s performance was perfectly synchronized with the performance of the benchmark.

Sharpe ratio measures the potential reward offered by a mutual fund relative to its risk level. The ratio uses a fund's standard deviation and its excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. The higher the Sharpe ratio, the better the fund's historical risk-adjusted performance.

Standard deviation represents the degree to which an investment’s performance has varied from its average performance over a particular time period.

Tracking error measures the extent to which a manager’s performance mimics that of a benchmark. The value is the standard deviation of the difference between a fund’s performance and a benchmark’s performance.

Upside capture measures a fund’s replication of its benchmark during periods of positive returns. During periods of positive benchmark returns, an upside capture ratio greater than 100% reflects product performance greater than the benchmark and an upside capture ratio less than 100% reflects performance less than the benchmark.

data.title: Nebius Vs. Navitas: Two Nvidia Darlings With Diverging AI-Driven Fortunes
data.description:
Navitas (NASDAQ:NVTS) and Nebius (NASDAQ:NBIS) have emerged as AI-winning investments this year. Although both companies operate across different components of the AI value chain, both have captured increasing investor attention due to their respective associations with industry leader Nvidia (NVDA). Yet only one is supported by durable underlying business fundamentals, in our opinion, while the other faces a risk profile that remains skewed to the downside.

Specifically, power semiconductor provider Navitas has gained exceptional market interest as of late. The company had disclosed in late May that it’s been chosen as one of the key suppliers of GaN and SiC power solutions for Nvidia’s next-generation 800V High Voltage Data Center (HVDC) architecture. Following the announcement, the stock exhibited a multifold upsurge, reflecting investors’ amplified optimism over Navitas’ prospective share in emerging AI infrastructure opportunities.

Meanwhile, Nebius’ ascent began last year when Nvidia disclosed a partial stake in the company. Yet, the company’s consistent delivery of underlying business fundamental improvements has effectively overtaken the Nvidia halo in recent months. Particularly after its Q1 earnings outperformance, markets have started to latch onto Nebius’ delivery of strong internal execution. This is corroborated by Nebius’ robust AI monetization improvements based on its robust Q1 results and accelerating Q2 outlook. The tangible, fundamental support accordingly conveys value accretion for the stock that’s underpinned by an optimized organic growth model, rather than superficial confidence that rides on the coattails of key investor Nvidia’s AI success.

Admittedly, both Navitas and Nebius are currently trading at a substantial valuation multiple premiums on a relative basis to their respective peers with comparative growth profiles. However, we believe Nebius continues to demonstrate a more compelling case for further upside, while Navitas’ valuation premium lacks durability at current levels. Our view is corroborated by the fundamental catalysts that continue to support Nebius’ organic growth and margin expansion trajectory as discussed earlier, alongside its management’s clear alignment with long-term shareholder interests.

This compares with Navitas’ more challenging operating landscape due to company- and industry-specific headwinds, spanning extended cyclical declines in its core EV, solar, and industrial end-markets. The set-up continues to limit visibility into the company’s recovery outlook, despite early signs of penetration into emerging AI infrastructure opportunities. The recent wave of insider selling from executive-level management following Navitas’ multifold stock upsurge since late May also raises questions about their commitment to maximizing long-term shareholder value.

Taken together, we believe Nebius remains the better investment at current levels, with an opportunity for further upside despite the stock already trading at a relative premium over its comparable peers. The following analysis will delve into the quantitative and qualitative drivers behind the respective valuation upsurges in both Navitas and Nebius stocks to gauge their respective forward prospects.

Nebius: Fundamentally Driven Execution

AI has been a key valuation-driving theme since the advent of OpenAI’s ChatGPT in late 2022. The solution has not only turned generative AI into a global sensation amongst mass market users but it’s also unleashed a transformation cycle across all industries. This has accordingly spurred investors to recalibrate their views on the growth prospects of key components across the AI value chain—particularly AI infrastructure providers that represent the backbone of related developments. Specifically, AI transformation has underpinned a slew of upward re-rates across tech stocks in recent years, especially as its potential to unlock new addressable markets (TAM) and ensuing incremental cash flow accretion becomes increasingly clear.

Yet underneath the qualitative thematic support, company-specific fundamental performance remains at the heart of sustained value appreciation. Based on our retrospective analysis of 96 tech high-fliers across the NYSE and Nasdaq in 2023 and 2024 performed earlier this year, we found their commonality lies specifically in fundamental achievements. Specifically, stocks that have appreciated by more than 100% over each of 2023 and 2024 have demonstrated multifold revenue acceleration and/or multifold earnings expansion. The ones that I’ve recently undergone an earnings inflection from losses to profitability through organic operations, with evidence of sustained margin expansion at scale, were particular winners. This winning combination of fundamental traits was typically accompanied by proactive alignment between management’s tone-at-the-top and shareholder value maximization.

And Nebius exemplifies this profile for exponential value creation. Beyond Nebius’ thematic relevance in the accelerating AI transformation, the company has also delivered tangible fundamental support, reinforced by its competitive advantage in capturing market share through its full-stack business model. Specifically, its end-to-end AI infrastructure offering, which spans both software and hardware solutions, continues to resonate deeply with customers seeking scalability – a top priority across boardroom budgets following the post-pandemic era optimization trend. Not only do Nebius’ integrated software solutions enable significant cost efficiencies for customers, but they also allow for rapid time-to-deployment. Together, this delivers the scalability needed for customers to fully harness AI’s efficiency gains and stay both economically and technologically competitive in the increasingly urgent industry transformation:

Customers I believe, recognize the value of our infrastructure and software. We were able to provide reliable and scalable service. Our software enables customers to start accessing clusters with thousands of GPUs just in a matter of days and not weeks. And we heard from some of our core customers’ recognition of that.

Source: Nebius Q1'25 Earnings Call Transcript

And Nebius’ integrated software-hardware offerings are equally critical for improving its unit economics. Not only do Nebius’ software offerings enable higher margin revenues due to their lower marginal cost of deployment, but they also unlock a wider range of critical AI cloud infrastructure solutions for customers. This accordingly increases adoption at scale, which inadvertently improves Nebius’ competitive unit economics by optimizing its revenue per GPU.

Nebius’ long-standing partnership with Nvidia is another competitive advantage that furthers its fundamental prospects. Thanks to its strategic alignment with Nvidia, Nebius will be amongst the first to deploy the next-generation Blackwell Ultra GB300 NVL72 systems when they start shipping in Q3. The company was also chosen as the launch partner for Nvidia’s new Dynamo framework. Unveiled during Computex 2025, Dynamo was introduced to broaden Nvidia processors’ compatibility with non-Nvidia hardware. As a result, Nebius’ role as a launch partner for Dynamo is expected to further its reach into an even wider range of customers by catering to non-Nvidia workloads and unlock new monetization opportunities for its existing GPUs.

Taken together, the combination of Nebius’ full-stack offering and its customer-centric alignment accordingly reinforces its long-term growth acceleration and margin accretion prospects. This is particularly critical for the company, given its more conservative depreciation schedule compared to peers like CoreWeave (CRWV). Specifically, Nebius currently adopts an accelerated full-year depreciation model. This compares to the typical five-to-six-year cycle observed at peers like CoreWeave. The accelerated depreciation model has essentially limited the buffer in Nebius’ bottom-line. In order to overcome this shortfall, Nebius must ensure disciplined cost management and margin-accretive execution while expanding market share. This continues to be demonstrated through Nebius’ prioritization of scalability as the key value proposition in its full-stack offerings, which reinforces overall visibility into its profitability trajectory.

This is corroborated by management’s guidance for adjusted EBITDA to turn positive by the second half of 2025, which signals a critical earnings inflection for Nebius. Achieving this fundamental milestone will not only validate Nebius’ differentiated AI strategy and execution but also improve confidence in its GAAP profitability outlook and cash flow growth prospects. Coupled with contracted visibility into Nebius’ multifold revenue growth over the next 12 months, its combined fundamental strength potentially underpins exponential value appreciation for the stock from current levels.

Navitas: Underappreciated Nvidia Deal Risks

Meanwhile, Navitas’ stock surge this year was comparatively steep and quick. The multifold gains were primarily driven by renewed market interest after the company announced its collaboration with Nvidia in late May. As a selected supplier of GaN and SiC power solutions for Nvidia’s next-generation 800V HVDC architecture, Navitas has gained newfound validation for its potential share of the multi-trillion-dollar AI infrastructure market.

However, underneath the headline catalyst are protracted fundamental challenges in Navitas’ underlying business. This causes us to turn incrementally cautious about the sustainability of Navitas’ eye-watering, multifold rally in recent weeks.

Looking back at Navitas’ earnings results in the past year, the company has struggled to overcome stiffening cyclical headwinds in its core solar, EV, and industrial end-markets. According to management’s commentary, these segments have been weighed down by protracted inventory corrections, dampening Navitas’ revenue growth and straining its overall financial position. This is evident in the extended string of revenue declines observed in Navitas over the past three quarters, with management anticipating further deterioration in the remainder of the year based on forward guidance.

This is further exacerbated by Navitas’ exposure to the ongoing U.S.-China tariff standoff. Specifically, its smaller mix of SiC revenues today is primarily generated from sales to China. But Navitas’ SiC solutions are primarily manufactured in the U.S., then subsequently shipped to China for packaging and final distribution in the region. As a result, the segment is prone to China’s retaliatory tariffs levied on shipments from the U.S. – a rate that had reached 125% prior to the temporary wind-down to 10% during the 90-day pause that’ll extend through mid-August. Admittedly, Navitas’ SiC revenue mix remains comparatively nominal to its core GaN sales. But the ongoing tariff uncertainties remain an added risk overhang, nonetheless, which only weighs further on the company’s already deteriorating fundamentals.

And even as Navitas expands its product roadmap with new GaN and SiC offerings tailored for AI data center applications, its actual market share in the emerging segment remains modest compared to its broader semiconductor peers. This is evident in uncertainties pertaining to the scope of Navitas’ participation in the upcoming Nvidia partnership, which, in our opinion, remains underappreciated by investors today.

Specifically, Nvidia had identified multiple certified silicon suppliers in its press release related to the next-generation 800V HVDC architecture. They include industry juggernauts like ROHM (OTCPK:ROHCF / OTCPK:ROHCY), STMicroelectronics (STM), and Texas Instruments (TXN). Yet Nvidia has stopped short of providing further details on how it’ll allocate sourcing across these suppliers. For now, we only know Navitas will be providing GaN/SiC power solutions. But given its comparatively smaller scale of operations, it’s highly unlikely it’ll be the sole supplier of said components. As a result, we believe the Nvidia partnership would introduce margin compression risks to Navitas, which shouldn’t be overlooked. Given the list of other disclosed silicon providers for Nvidia’s next-generation 800V HVDC architecture are industry heavyweights with marked scale and cost advantages, they’re likely able to offer comparable power solutions at competitive prices. This could accordingly expose Navitas to inferior pricing arrangements as it vies for its share of emerging AI-driven demand for GaN and SiC power semiconductor solutions.

This challenge is compounded by Navitas’ long-standing streak of operating losses. Without a proven track record of profitability, and potentially newfound margin compression risks, the company faces increased pressure in funding growth investments while managing imminent margin erosion. Limited scale benefits stemming from protracted weakness in core end-markets also threaten to further squeeze liquidity, limiting Navitas’ financial flexibility at a time of increasing competition in the global AI arms race.

Taken together, the risk to Navitas’ unit economics ahead remains significant. If it cannot secure a meaningful share in the build-out of Nvidia’s 800V HVDC architecture, or if it must further sacrifice its already thin margins to do so, then Navitas will likely need further external financing. This means, Navitas may be forced to exercise its at-the-market (ATM) equity offering arrangement, which permits issuance of up to $50 million worth of additional shares at the company’s discretion. That’s about a 4% dilution based on the stock’s current price (June 5), while also signaling deeper concerns about Navitas’ ability to fund longer-term growth.

Adding to investors’ angst is the recent pattern of insider selling by executive-level management. Since the Nvidia partnership was announced on May 21 and after the stock’s immediate multifold upsurge, executive-level management – particularly board members, directors, and Navitas’ CEO – have collectively disposed of more than 4.5 million shares. Meanwhile, Form-144 filings indicate that only about 46% of intended share sales by the cohort of insiders disclosed since May 21 have been executed to date, highlighting further disposals to come:

While the Nvidia partnership is a step in the right direction by adding support to Navitas’ long-term growth story, the heavy insider selling activity sends a conflicting message. It suggests that even as the company faces strengthened AI prospects, management may be more focused on taking advantage of short-term price gains rather than maximizing long-term shareholder value.

Although the Nvidia partnership appears promising for Navitas on the surface, we believe it does little to mitigate the company’s broader operational challenges. Persistent weakness across its core verticals, potential incremental margin pressures from larger competitors in AI data center opportunities, and the looming risk of further leverage and share dilution collectively worsen Navitas’ risk profile. Coupled with the recent slew of heavy insider selling, the recent upsurge in Navitas stock may not be as sustainable as the headlines suggest.

Navitas: Fundamental and Price Considerations

Despite persistent revenue declines and deteriorating margins, Navitas still trades at more than 15x NTM sales, which significantly exceeds levels observed across other semiconductor names that exhibit a similar growth profile:

This suggests substantial optimism priced into the stock, especially over the underlying business’ growth prospects stemming from its recent partnership with Nvidia. Yet considering our foregoing analysis on the underlying risks of the arrangement, spanning uncertainties to the extent of Navitas’ participation in Nvidia’s scale-out of the next-generation 800V HVDC architecture and potential exposure to adverse pricing due to competition, we believe the stock’s frothy valuation at current levels remain prone to elevated downside risks. This is further pressured by the ongoing inventory correction cycle at Navitas’ core EV, solar, and industrial end-markets, which provide limited alleviation to its deteriorating fundamental prospects.

As a result, we believe Navitas’ valuation premium reflects the market’s mispricing of the looming execution risks, especially with incremental consideration of the tariff overhang that’s facing its China-focused SiC sales and pressure from significant insider selling. Taken together, we remain incrementally cautious about the durability of Navitas’ valuation at current levels.

Nebius: Fundamental and Price Considerations

Meanwhile, compared to Navitas, Nebius also shows a comparatively modest degree of valuation premium over its comparable peers in AI infrastructure solutions at current levels:

Yet, Nebius’ multifold growth prospect anticipated over the next 24 months provides a buffer. Specifically, the growth outlook, partially underpinned by long-term contracts to ensure visibility, demonstrates organic market share expansion to ensure the scale required for sustained margin accretion. The anticipated inflection to positive adjusted EBITDA by year-end also represents an added upside catalyst, in our opinion, which could reinforce further re-rate prospects for the Nebius stock from current levels.

Final Thoughts

While both Nebius and Navitas have seen their respective valuations surge to premiums relative to their comparable peers this year, we believe Nebius remains better positioned for a further upward re-rate.

For Navitas, the Nvidia partnership has certainly brought renewed investor enthusiasm and market validation. However, this headline catalyst does little to alleviate the company’s underlying fundamental challenges, driven by persistent weakness in its core end markets and slimming margins. Coupled with the recent slew of executive-level management selling off the stock, there’s limited evidence that the company’s business plan is aligned with sustained shareholder value creation. Taken together, we believe the downside risks facing Navitas at current levels remain in place.

In contrast, Nebius’ valuation strength is anchored in a far more robust and consistent set of tangible, fundamental-driven catalysts. Its expansion into AI infrastructure is corroborated by the clear execution of margin-expanding strategies, market share gains, and a disciplined approach to cost and profitability. This is critical for optimizing returns to offset the accelerating pace of underlying hardware depreciation that follows the industry’s rapid cadence of tech advancements.

While both stocks have appreciated significantly, Nebius’ focus on driving higher-margin, scalable solutions with its full-stack business model provides a clearer path to sustained, long-term growth. This also reinforces visibility into its pathway to profitability. Coupled with management’s disciplined execution and proven adaptability, Nebius continues to stand out as an emerging AI winner.

data.title: Nu Skin: Shares May Have Finally Printed A Multi-Year Bottom
data.description:
Intro

Our most recent commentary on Nu Skin Enterprises, Inc. (NYSE:NUS) was in May 2022, when we backed the company's transition strategy to eventually return the direct-selling beauty & wellness company to growth. Our optimism, however, came with a cautious tone, considering the lack of technical support beneath the prevailing share price at the time. Deteriorating forward-looking EPS revision trends were another warning that we hope investors heeded.

As we see from Nu Skin's long-term technical chart, once the move accelerated to the downside, due to the lack of any type of meaningful technical support, lower lows & lower highs became Nu Skin's reality rather quickly. The aggressive down move (resulting in shares losing 80 %+ of their value over the past 3 years) was prompted by both negative revenue & profit growth, stifling margins in the process.

Shares of NUS seem to have finally found some solid support as they have come right back down to successfully test the stock's 2002 & 2008 lows. Therefore, taking the above into account & how the stock's long-term ADX trend following indicator seems to be finally coming off its highs, we would expect some consolidation in NUS stock over the near term.

Therefore, let's go through the investment case for Nu Skin to see if the stock is a viable 'value play' that deserves a spot on our watchlist.

Q1 Earnings Trends For Fiscal Year 2025

Nu Skin announced its first quarter earnings for the present fiscal year on 5/8/2025. Revenues of $364.5 million came in better than expected, with adjusted EPS of $0.23 also surprising to the upside. Furthermore, gross margin gains along with reduced selling & general and administrative costs bode well for quality control. Latin America remains a hot spot for Nu Skin concerning growth, while the untapped Indian 'entrepreneur hungry' market is expected to undergo significant development, most likely by the end of this year and then into fiscal 2026.

The recent sale of Mavely for a $176 million cash gain has boosted Nu Skin's cash position and reduced outstanding debt to $155 million. The boost in liquidity is encouraging, given management's plan to double down on what has been working for the company. To this point, the CEO knows full well that the best lineup of products combined with an excellent compensation plan (where leaders are rewarded generously) is the only way to succeed in a highly competitive direct-selling environment.

Investors Seeking New Growth Path

At the end of the day, Nu Skin's resellers (being business owners in their own right) are essentially costs for the company on the income statement (Compensation plan pay-outs). Although margins have been increasing, you feel that stronger revenue growth rates will be needed to surmount these costs, thus ensuring Nu Skin leaders get compensated generously over the long term. Macro trends in each market are always very important, so in India, for example, management must ensure its beauty and wellness offerings are priced correctly so that both Nu Skin & local entrepreneurs can be compensated adequately.

To improve the company's value proposition, Nu Skin is introducing 'Prysm iO' (an Intelligent wellness device that measures carotenoid levels in the skin). Management anticipates the product will significantly increase engagement levels with customers over time.

Prysm iO is a palm-sized device that accurately measures the carotenoid levels in your skin via the fingertip to provide insight to consumers about their overall antioxidant score. As more consumers get their intelligent antioxidant score with Prysm iO, we anticipate nutritional supplement purchases and subscriptions will increase, driving incremental revenue and retention, which will result in a greater customer lifetime value and will also provide greater opportunities for our sales force to grow their independent beauty and wellness businesses.

Nu Skin Is Self Funded From A Cash-Flow Standpoint

When we delve into Nu Skin's valuation multiples below, we see that the stock is currently trading with very attractive sales, book & cash-flow multiples. Furthermore, trailing free cash flow (which wouldn't have been affected by the Mavely sale) of $1.33 per share is easily covering the revised dividend payout ($0.24). Suffice it to say, Nu Skin, from a cash-flow standpoint, is self-funded, which is key concerning being able to grow the company organically over time.

Assets & Sales Trading On The Cheap

Nu Skin's trailing sales multiple of 0.24 & trailing book multiple of 0.52 will attract value investors, especially if revenue growth can now begin in earnest. These multiples come in well behind the company's 5-year averages & sector medians in general. Although starting from a low quarterly base (Reported revenues of $364.5 million in Q1), management has guided north of $370 million in revenues in Q2, so hitting this target would be encouraging.

Furthermore, the fact that Nu Skin's assets are primarily made up of cash, inventory & 'Net Property, Plant & Equipment' gives plenty of merit to the reported price-to-book ratio. Shareholder equity came in at $754 million at the end of Q2, but this doesn't tell the whole story of how cheap this company is trading for. Let me explain.

Elevated Treasury Stock

Although management used proceeds from the Mavely sale to bring down the company's debt in Q1, Nu Skin's total liabilities still amounted to $637 million at the end of the company's most recent quarter. Value investors may be concerned with these debts, considering Nu Skin's trailing negative earnings almost make up the company's equity ($754 million). Nevertheless, Nu Skin's $1.56 billion of treasury stock in the equity part of the balance sheet brings positive considerations.

Why? Treasury stock is stock up to now, which has been bought back by the company but has yet to be retired. This means the company can reissue these shares sometime in the future if needed. Therefore, given the fact that Treasury Stock appears as a negative on the balance sheet, such a sizable number (which is what we want) can end up distorting reported debt & profitability ratios (Debt to equity & ROE, for example). Case in point. Nu Skin has very cheap assets & once they start producing growth in earnest, we would expect value investors to begin stepping in.

Conclusion

To sum up, we rate Nu Skin Enterprises a 'Hold' at present due to its compelling valuation, encouraging technicals, and sustainable free cash flow trends. Let's see if macro trends can begin cooperating for the company in its key markets. We look forward to continued coverage.

data.title: Musk's Political Pivot Is A Textbook Dip-Buying Opportunity For Tesla
data.description:
Tesla, Inc. (NASDAQ:TSLA) shares plunged 14% yesterday following CEO Elon Musk’s apparent pivot against U.S. President Donald J. Trump. In just one day, Musk went from being one of the strongest advocates of this administration to bringing forward strong political accusations against the sitting U.S. President (see Tweets below from the X account of Tesla’s CEO).

I think this represents a great dip buying opportunity, since Tesla’s fundamentals have not changed at all. If anything, I believe Musk is realigning Tesla’s brand with his original user base, providing a catalyst for the stock. Tesla remains a momentum stock, with a premium valuation that is in my view tied to the presence of its founder, Elon Musk. Readers who cannot stand high volatility and those who are comfortable investing only in stocks with cheap valuations would do better to avoid this stock.

How Musk’s political pivot could help “realign” Tesla’s brand

It is no secret that Tesla’s customer base tends to be high-income consumers, affluent professionals, and tech enthusiasts. Other studies also show how Tesla’s historical customer base tends to be liberal and left-leaning, with the environment as a key topic for buying an EV.

Many have noticed how Musk’s support for Trump could alienate Tesla’s customer base. This was one of the reasons the stock rallied following disappointing Q1 2025 earnings when Elon Musk announced he would refocus his efforts on Tesla away from politics.

I think this apparent pivot away from the current administration will help re-aligning Tesla’s brand with its historical customer base, which may indeed have felt alienated by Musk’s vicinity to Trump.

Ultimately, Tesla cars are always the best-rated among EVs. I think that like in any other consumer category, automotive included, product quality and competitive pricing oversee any political considerations, in the long term. Research confirms the elements that consumers look at before choosing a car include reliability, safety, and fuel efficiency. This is not to say politics cannot hinder a brand (it can!), but that as long as Musk distances himself from politics (as he is now doing), the quality and competitiveness of Tesla’s cars are what matters.

And all that is without mentioning Tesla’s other businesses, including Energy, Services, Robotaxis, and Robotics. Some of these may be even less subject to political swings, as they have a strong B2B component (Energy, Robotics) or they are tied to the existing customer base (Services).

Can Trump hinder Tesla’s future?

The only way I can see the Trump administration hurting Tesla’s business is by removing EV subsidies. This, however, is something that was already present in the “big, beautiful tax bill” that is currently to be discussed in the U.S. Senate. Musk’s apparent “feud” with Trump could at most have the effect of confirming what should have already been priced in by the market: the removal of EV subsidies. In other terms, it does not represent new negative news that warrants any correction in Tesla’s stock.

Trump has hinted at other ways to “get back” at Musk, including removing environmental subsidies. However, most of these threats do not concern Tesla directly, but rather SpaceX (SPACE) (which has significant contracts with NASA).

Personally, I also think that whatever short-term political decision Trump may take, it will be most likely cancelled in the future. This is an idea I have discussed in many of my previous macro articles, most recently arguing that Trump now has effectively no choice except pivoting on his questionable and unconventional economic policies, especially concerning trade tariffs.

Politics remains impossible to predict by definition (something I will cover in the next section), but as long as readers believe the U.S. will go back to a “normal” of being managed based on mainstream free market, capitalistic economics and without political pivots taking place every two weeks, I think entering a position in Tesla at current levels will result in outsized gains.

Risks to my thesis and why I would only enter TSLA long term

As of data available at the time of writing, Tesla’s car sales are still seeing a slump in May 2025 across China and European markets. The launch of a revamped Tesla Model Y “blurs” these results, as Tesla is still phasing out older Models. However, whether a recovery in deliveries will be swift (or happen at all) remains to be seen.

While I deem likely Trump’s unconventional policies will be overturned, either as a result of impeachment, sidelining by Congress, or legal action in courts, I may be wrong. Trump may remain in office and enact his policies well until 2028, and Tesla may suffer for a long time due to Musk’s new apparent anti-Trump political stance.

Regardless of politics, Tesla is also a volatile stock, trading at a rich premium and subject to swift changes in momentum and sentiment. Personally, I remain bullish on Tesla, but I would only enter a position if I was willing to hold for at least a decade from now.

As I have discussed in my last coverage for this company, which was highly debated and generated almost 200 comments, I think Tesla can easily grow into its (admittedly very rich) valuation. I actually think Tesla has the potential to grow much further than its current valuation, and I see a 5X-10X potential in the long term as achievable (as discussed in my stretched bullish scenarios of my previous coverage). For details on my reasoning about Tesla’s valuation, I encourage readers to refer to that article.

However, this is admittedly not a stock for everyone and I encourage readers who are wary of volatility or do not feel comfortable holding stocks at premium valuations to stay away from Tesla, even after the dip.

Conclusion

While my personal experience is in no way of statistical relevance, I have a personal anecdote to share as a conclusion to this article. I recently was shopping for a new car and tried traditional automakers such as Audi and Porsche. Tesla (or any other EV, for that matter) was not on my radar at all, but I still ended up trying a Model 3 and a Model Y out of curiosity. The only thing I can say is that trying a Tesla felt like stepping a couple of decades into the future, compared to the last models the German rivals offer today. I ended up ordering a Model Y, which I will receive later in June (what also helped is Tesla’s 0% interest financing in my base country, Switzerland - probably a sign that deliveries are indeed not so great for Q2).

Readers who follow me will know that I have been very critical about the current U.S. administration and its economic policies, which I see akin to socialism. This is despite having been a sympathizer of Trump ahead of elections in 2024. Yet, I have decided to buy a Tesla just because the car felt… better than others. This is to say, consumers end up preferring the best products in the market and political considerations may often be overblown. Not only does existing consumer research confirm this, but I have now experienced this on myself.

I remain bullish on Tesla long term and reiterate my Buy rating with a long-term investment horizon, seeing this recent dip as a great entry point.
