data.title: Bio-Techne: Slow Growth Amidst Healthcare Policy, MAHA Movement
data.description:
Summary

Bio-Techne Corporation (NASDAQ:TECH) operates in the life science reagents, testing, and diagnostic space. Its products aid in drug discovery, clinical testing, and diagnosis with a focus on proteins, antibodies, and immunoassays. Primary consumers of their products include researchers in academia, biotech research companies, and the pharmaceutical industry. No customer represents more than 10% of annual sales; therefore, its business consists of a diverse set of clients both domestically and around the globe. But along with the variable clientele comes a higher risk set. Economic downturns dampen the lab testing industry as a whole, and Bio-Techne has exposure to changes in China/U.S. trade relations. Moreover, with the current White House administration’s focus on protectionist policy, lower drug prices, FDA revamps via Secretary Kennedy, and the MAHA movement, TECH will see a mixed bag of both revenue growth & deceleration as the healthcare shake-out continues. With an expensive valuation, a complex business, and a complicated future, TECH is a sell.

Background: Annual Report & Strategy

Bio-Techne has an encouraging business model: gain exposure to testing & diagnostics through strategic acquisitions, invest in high-grade “GMP” (Good Manufacturing Practice) reagents, and develop new technologies for its two segments: protein sciences and diagnostics/genomics. The protein sciences segment represented 72% of fiscal 2024 sales (as per the Annual Report), and has a wide array of solutions, including but not limited to cytokines, antibodies, tissue culture, cell selection, manual & automated protein analysis, and immunoassays. These solutions are targeted toward various diseases/illnesses, including cancer, autoimmune, diabetes, hypertension, obesity, inflammation, neurological, and kidney disease. The diagnostics & genomics segment includes advanced cell diagnostics, molecular diagnostics, and reagents within clinical settings and blood chemistry. For example, management boasts of their “ExoDx Prostate” test, which is a urine-based assay for cancer detection, with over 100,000 tests as of fiscal 2023. The list of products and applications is not exhaustive.

Roughly 43% of sales originate outside the U.S., which opens the door to international risks as well as growth. Specifically, management seeks to expand into China & India – among other developing nations – but the Trump 2.0 impact on trade relations poses a threat to said growth. Moreover, the post-pandemic macroeconomic risks in China persist, which hurts R&D spending in the Asia Pacific region. Domestically, Bio-Techne’s clientele includes academic researchers, biotech companies, and other players in the pharmaceutical industry. There has been ongoing uncertainty when it comes to scientific research funding (we could point to the current administration as a primary cause), leading to a pull-back on laboratory research ventures. As a result, TECH shares have demonstrated lackluster performance since January 2025, dropping over 30 percent.

Government Policy Headwinds

Per the “Risk Factors” section of the Annual Report, we can glean some important details regarding the macro trends hitting TECH. In particular, R&D spending, the China factor, and an FDA makeover present certain challenges to the company’s success:

“One of our strategies is to expand geographically, particularly in China, India and in developing countries, both through distribution and direct operations. This subjects us to a number of risks, including international economic, political, and labor conditions […]” (pp. 18).

Later on in the section, we come to the healthcare impact on Bio-Techne; intuitively, one might say that the policy set by the U.S. government also sets the direction for TECH performance:

“Our Protein Sciences segment products are sold primarily to research scientists at pharmaceutical and biotechnology companies and at university and government research institutions […] Our Diagnostics and Genomics segment products are intended primarily for the medical diagnostics market, which relies largely on government healthcare-related policies and funding […] Additionally, the U.S. government’s plans to manage prescription drug prices, as well as its recently announced intention to regulate lab developed tests, may impact the customers and industries we serve by increasing the cost of commercializing and/or limiting the profitability of commercialized products. All of these payor actions and changes may have a material adverse effect on revenue and earnings associated with our diagnostic products.” (pp. 18-19)

Before we draw conclusions, it is important to note that the 2024 Q4 report was released in August 2024, which means the discussion above occurred prior to Trump 2.0. Even during the Biden administration, Bio-Techne was facing some headwinds on both the prescription drug and the lab testing fronts. It’s worth drilling down on the latter, as the lab testing climate appears to change at the whims of the Executive branch. According to an April 2025 Deseret article, “Judge foils FDA plan to regulate lab-developed tests” by Lois Collins, a U.S. District Court judge struck down a Biden-era ruling by the FDA to treat laboratory tests like medical device tests. In essence, the ruling involved thousands of various lab tests that deal with – among other conditions – cancers that require LDTs (laboratory-developed tests) for identification and diagnosis. The ruling seems like an ancillary win for TECH, but the question of lab test oversight remains unanswered, as the Texas court punted the question back to the Department of Health and Human Services.

The new HHS Secretary – RFK, Jr. – has well-known qualms about the FDA (which would definitely include lab testing regulation), prescription drug prices, and healthcare research: all of which fall under Bio-Techne’s purview. The “MAHA” Executive Order dated February 13th, 2025 signifies both President Trump’s and Secretary Kennedy’s combined desire to root out the chronic disease and reduce “over-reliance on medication and treatments”. The Order also calls for more transparency across the healthcare system, and recent so-called “gold-standard” scientific research, and coupled with the May 12th “Most-Favored-Nation” Prescription Drug Pricing Order, we have a trifecta of policy events that, frankly, has already affected laboratory testing & research across the country. Plainly stated we have directives for less medication, more targeted research, and lower prices. I will take it one step further, and make the leap: HHS policy will cap any upside in TECH shares for the next 12-24 months.

Performance Through 2025 Q3

While I may see a short opportunity in TECH shares, there may very well be reason for optimism. One source of this optimism comes from the most recent earnings commentary and CEO Kim Kelderman. Even though China continues to be, in the words of the CEO, “still challenging” (per the Q3 conference call), there are anticipated tailwinds for the GMP reagents segment with high single-digit revenue growth, and double-digit revenue growth for Wilson Wolf. Although as a whole the business grew sales by 6%, there remain muted trends among academia, NIH funding, and pharma growth. Regarding the research trend in academics and the influence behind HHS, consider the quote by the CEO below:

“[…] our US academic customers are facing uncertainty around the future funding of their research projects. This can impact purchase decisions, particularly around capital equipment. Another policy shift that has been announced by the new secretary of the Department of Health and Human Services is getting a much higher priority around combating chronic disease […] Once the dust settles around the overall level of NIH funding, Bio-Techne stands to benefit from the NIH grants geared towards these diseases as our product portfolio is perfectly aligned with those research areas.”

Dissecting the Q3 conference call and the Annual Report, it seems clear that throughout 2024 and into Q3 2025, academia revenues have been and will continue to be soft. Similarly, a reduction in NIH funding would likely be a blow to biotech & pharma research activity. But it is not all bad news: management welcomes the opportunity to embrace the MAHA movement and believes the company will gain more sales from the targeted research intended by NIH. Generally speaking, there are two modes of thought: either TECH will be at the forefront of HHS research (drawing more sales via testing and diagnostics in an attempt to fight chronic disease), or shares of TECH will lose out along with the pullback in R&D spending across academia and pharmaceuticals/lab testing. For the time being, I expect the latter.

Valuation, and Risks to a Short

There is still growth happening at Bio-Techne, and revenue growth – no matter how small – hurts a short position. Yet from 2023 to 2024, TECH saw its EBIT drop nearly 13% from $281 million to $245 million (as per Seeking Alpha). Moreover, through Q3 2025 the trailing twelve-month EBIT stands at $224 million. While the revenues are going up, earnings are going down. From this, we can infer there is a downtrend with the business: China represents 9% of the company’s revenues and still presents a macro problem. The changes going on at HHS will continue to drag on Bio-Techne’s market opportunity. The Wilson Wolf acquisition mentioned above will be completed by 2027, and although the future subsidiary is looking at double-digit growth for 2025, TECH is obligated to pay 4.4x sales for the rest of the company. It’s a double-edged sword, in that Wilson Wolf will contribute positively in the gene & cell therapy space, yet the better they do, the more TECH has to pay, up to a cap of $1 billion. At $48/share, TECH itself is trading at a 6.2x forward price/sales multiple, and a 20.6x forward EV/EBIT multiple…both are overpriced compared to medians of 3.3x and 14.6x, respectively. At a 4.4x sales multiple – roughly the same value management is paying for Wilson Wolf – TECH should be trading around $34/share.

While I see a potentially 29% downside to TECH shares, I am willing to admit – again – that sales are growing, albeit slowly, and the Wilson Wolf acquisition could pay off in the long run (the deal should be completed by 2027). Either of these outcomes or a more disciplined approach to expenses on the part of management, could ruin a short position. Yet, I believe the short risks are mitigated by the overwhelming trends away from academic research spending and toward specifically targeted healthcare policy (i.e., MAHA). Management believes they will be the winner once the healthcare dust settles, as more testing could be required to take aim at chronic disease and childhood diseases. I understand there are competing cross-currents of growth versus deceleration. For better or worse, I take the deceleration view.

Conclusion

Bio-Techne operates in a complex space and has a decent business model. There are thousands of applications for its protein segment, and the diagnostic testing space may have room to grow over time, as the Wilson Wolf acquisition plays out. Yet, TECH shares are trading at an expensive valuation based on both revenues and earnings, implying room to the downside. The macro trends are almost certainly cause for concern, as foreign growth – China in particular – remains subdued. The “Make America Health Again” movement will shake up the sector for at least the next 12 to 24 months, affecting academic research & pharma investment, and in turn, hitting Bio-Techne’s growth avenues. While the company may continue to see single-digit growth, it will be capped by policy and international headwinds.
